---
title: ""
author: "Giorgio Arcara"
date: ""
format:
  beamer:
    theme: metropolis
    keep-tex: true
    pdf-engine: xelatex
incremental: false
header-includes: |
  \setbeamerfont{title}{size=\large}
  \usepackage{hyperref}
  \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
  \usepackage{tikz}
  \usetikzlibrary{arrows.meta, positioning, shapes.geometric, fit, backgrounds, calc}
  \usepackage{booktabs}
  \usepackage{xcolor}
  \usepackage{array}
  \usepackage{colortbl}
  \definecolor{mblue}{RGB}{35,55,100}
  \definecolor{mred}{RGB}{160,30,30}
  \definecolor{mgreen}{RGB}{30,100,60}
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="beamer"}
```{=latex}
\title{Clinical Research Methodology\\
\vspace{0.5em}
{\normalsize \textbf{APPENDIX --- Linear and Logistic Regression}}}
\author{Giorgio Arcara,\\ University of Padua \\ IRCCS San Camillo, Venice}
\titlegraphic{
\vspace*{7cm}
\includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/LogoSanCamilloIRCCS_Unipd_alpha.png}
\hfill \includegraphics[scale=0.15]{../Figures/CC_license_3_0.png}
}
\maketitle
```
:::

# Introduction to Regression Models

## Why Regression?

\small

Regression is the most widely used statistical tool in clinical research for:

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Describing the relationship between a dependent variable and one or more predictors
\item Statistically controlling for confounding variables
\item Predicting an outcome from a set of variables
\item Quantifying the magnitude of the effect of a predictor \emph{while holding others constant}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4em}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!15,text width=3.2cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=0.9cm},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
\node[box] (x1) at (-3.5,0) {Predictors\\$X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_p$};
\node[box] (mod) at (0,0)   {Regression\\Model};
\node[box] (y)  at (3.5,0)  {Dependent\\variable $Y$};
\draw[arr] (x1)--(mod); \draw[arr] (mod)--(y);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

\vspace{0.3em}
\small Two distinct questions: \textbf{(1) explanation} --- which predictors are associated with $Y$ and by how much? \textbf{(2) prediction} --- how accurately can I estimate $Y$ for a new subject?

## Linear vs Logistic Regression: When to Use Them

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  box/.style={draw,rounded corners,fill=mblue!15,text width=4.2cm,
              align=center,font=\footnotesize,minimum height=1.3cm},
]
\node[box] (lin) at (-2.8,0) {
  \textbf{Linear Regression}\\[4pt]
  \footnotesize $Y$ continuous\\
  E.g.\ MMSE score, hippocampal volume,\\
  depression scale score
};
\node[box] (log) at (2.8,0) {
  \textbf{Logistic Regression}\\[4pt]
  \footnotesize $Y$ binary (0/1)\\
  E.g.\ MCI diagnosis yes/no,\\
  conversion to AD yes/no,\\
  treatment response yes/no
};
\node[font=\normalsize\bfseries,mblue] at (0,0) {vs};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.4em}
\small \textbf{Note}: extensions exist for ordinal outcomes (ordinal regression), counts (Poisson), time to event (Cox) --- but linear and logistic cover the majority of situations in neuropsychology.
```

# Linear Regression

## The Model

\small

$$Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \beta_2 X_{2i} + \ldots + \beta_p X_{pi} + \varepsilon_i$$

where $\varepsilon_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ (errors i.i.d., normal distribution).

\vspace{0.4em}

**Interpretation of coefficients** $\beta_j$:

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item $\beta_0$: expected value of $Y$ when all predictors = 0 (intercept)
\item $\beta_j$: expected change in $Y$ for a one-unit increase in $X_j$,\\ \textbf{while holding all other predictors constant}
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.4em}

**Estimation**: ordinary least squares (OLS) --- minimizes $\sum_i (Y_i - \hat{Y}_i)^2$.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Fit measures**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item $R^2$: proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by the model
\item Adjusted $R^2$: penalizes for the number of predictors
\item RMSE (Root Mean Squared Error): mean error in units of $Y$
\end{itemize}

<!--
## Visualization: Simple Regression

```{=latex}
% [SUGGESTED IMAGE: scatterplot with regression line and confidence bands,
%  e.g., age vs MMSE score in MCI patients]
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.88]
  \draw[thick,->] (0,0) -- (6.5,0) node[right,font=\small]{Age (years)};
  \draw[thick,->] (0,0) -- (0,4.5) node[above,font=\small]{MMSE};

  % Axis labels
  \foreach \x/\lbl in {1/60, 2/65, 3/70, 4/75, 5/80}
    \node[below,font=\tiny] at (\x*1.1,-0.15) {\lbl};
  \foreach \y/\lbl in {0.5/20, 1.5/22, 2.5/24, 3.5/26, 4.2/28}
    \node[left,font=\tiny] at (-0.1,\y) {\lbl};

  % Confidence band
  \fill[mblue!10] plot[smooth] coordinates
    {(1.1,3.8)(2.2,3.3)(3.3,2.7)(4.4,2.1)(5.5,1.5)} --
    plot[smooth,reverse path] coordinates
    {(1.1,3.2)(2.2,2.8)(3.3,2.3)(4.4,1.7)(5.5,1.1)};

  % Regression line
  \draw[thick,mblue] (1.1,3.5) -- (5.5,1.3);

  % Data points
  \foreach \x/\y in {
    1.2/3.7, 1.4/3.4, 1.8/3.6, 2.0/3.1, 2.3/2.9, 2.5/3.3,
    2.8/2.7, 3.1/2.5, 3.3/2.8, 3.6/2.2, 3.9/2.0, 4.1/2.4,
    4.3/1.9, 4.6/1.6, 4.8/2.0, 5.0/1.4, 5.3/1.2, 5.5/1.5
  }{
    \fill[mblue!70] (\x,\y) circle (2.2pt);
  }

  % Residual example
  \draw[mred,dashed] (3.1,2.5) -- (3.1,2.1);
  \node[right,font=\tiny,mred] at (3.15,2.3) {$\varepsilon_i$};

  \node[font=\footnotesize,mblue] at (4.5,3.8)
    {$\hat{Y} = 5.2 - 0.05 \cdot \text{Age}$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.4em}
\small Each point is a MCI patient. The line is the OLS estimate. The gray bands are the 95\% CI of the line. Residuals $\varepsilon_i$ are the vertical distances between points and the line.
```

-->
## Output of a Linear Regression (as it appears in an article)

```{=latex}
\vspace{-0.2em}
\begin{center}
\small
\textbf{Table 1.} Predictors of MMSE score in patients with MCI ($N = 120$)
\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Predictor} & $\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}}$ & \textbf{SE} & \textbf{95\% CI} & $\boldsymbol{p}$ \\
\midrule
Intercept           & 34.21 & 2.18 & [29.90, 38.52] & $<.001$ \\
\rowcolor{mblue!8}
Age (years)           & $-0.18$ & 0.04 & [$-0.26$, $-0.10$] & $<.001$ \\
Education (years)     & $+0.31$ & 0.07 & [$+0.17$, $+0.45$] & $<.001$ \\
\rowcolor{mblue!8}
Depression (GDS)    & $-0.22$ & 0.09 & [$-0.40$, $-0.04$] & $.018$  \\
Sex (F vs M)       & $-0.41$ & 0.48 & [$-1.36$, $+0.54$] & $.393$  \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{5}{l}{\footnotesize $R^2 = .38$; \; $R^2_{\text{adj}} = .36$; \; $F(4, 115) = 17.6$, $p < .001$} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.2em}
\footnotesize
\textbf{Reading}: Each additional year of age is associated with a lower MMSE score of 0.18 points ($p < .001$), holding education, depression and sex constant. Sex is not a significant predictor in this model ($p = .393$).
```

## How to Read Coefficients (Linear Regression)

\small

**Continuous predictor** (e.g., education):

$\hat{\beta} = +0.31$ means: for each additional year of education, the MMSE score increases by 0.31 points \emph{on average}, holding other predictors constant.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Binary categorical predictor** (e.g., sex F vs M):

$\hat{\beta} = -0.41$ means: women have an MMSE score on average 0.41 points lower than men, holding other predictors constant. (Not significant: $p = .393$.)

\vspace{0.4em}

**95\% confidence interval**: the interval that, if we repeated the study infinitely many times, would contain the true parameter 95\% of the time. If the CI \textbf{does not include zero} $\Rightarrow$ significant at $\alpha = 0.05$.

\vspace{0.4em}

\textbf{Beware of units}: $\hat{\beta}$ depends on the measurement units of the predictor. To compare the relative importance of predictors with different scales, use \textbf{standardized coefficients} ($\beta^*$).

# Logistic Regression

## The Problem with a Binary Dependent Variable
<!--
```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.88]
  \draw[thick,->] (0,-0.3) -- (6.5,-0.3) node[right,font=\small]{MMSE};
  \draw[thick,->] (0,-0.3) -- (0,3.8) node[above,font=\small]{AD Conversion (0/1)};
  \node[left,font=\tiny] at (-0.1,-0.3) {0};
  \node[left,font=\tiny] at (-0.1,3.3) {1};
  \draw[dashed,gray!50] (0,3.3) -- (6.2,3.3);

  % Data points at 0 and 1
  \foreach \x in {1.0,1.5,2.0,2.3,2.8,3.2}
    \fill[mred!70] (\x,3.3) circle (2.5pt);
  \foreach \x in {3.5,4.0,4.5,5.0,5.5,6.0}
    \fill[mblue!70] (\x,-0.3) circle (2.5pt);
  \foreach \x in {2.5,3.0,3.8,4.2}
    \fill[mblue!40] (\x,3.3) circle (2.5pt);
  \foreach \x in {1.8,2.2,3.3}
    \fill[mred!40] (\x,-0.3) circle (2.5pt);

  % Linear fit (bad)
  \draw[mred,thick,dashed] (0.5,3.8) -- (6.2,-0.5);
  \node[right,font=\tiny,mred] at (5.5,0.5) {Linear: values $>1$ and $<0$!};

  % Logistic curve (good)
  \draw[mblue,very thick] plot[smooth,domain=0.3:6.2,samples=60]
    (\x, {3.3/(1+exp(3*(\x-3.2)))});
  \node[right,font=\tiny,mblue, align=center] at (4.5,2.3) {Logistic:\\always in $[0,1]$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center>
\vspace{-0.4em}
\small Linear regression on $Y \in \{0,1\}$ can predict probabilities $<0$ or $>1$. \textbf{Logistic regression} directly models the probability of the event keeping it in $[0,1]$.
```
-->

## The Logistic Model

\small

$$\log\left(\frac{p_i}{1-p_i}\right) = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \ldots + \beta_p X_{pi}$$

The term on the left is the **logit** (log-odds): the logarithm of the ratio of the probability of the event to the probability of the non-event.

\vspace{0.3em}

Inverting:
$$p_i = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_{1i} + \ldots)}}$$

\vspace{0.3em}

**Estimation**: maximum likelihood (ML), not least squares.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Fit measures**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Log-likelihood, AIC, BIC (lower = better)
\item Nagelkerke's $R^2$, McFadden's $R^2$ (pseudo-$R^2$, not identical to linear $R^2$)
\item Hosmer-Lemeshow test (calibration)
\item AUC-ROC (discrimination)
\end{itemize}

## The Odds Ratio: Intuition

\small

The coefficient $\hat{\beta}_j$ of logistic regression is not interpreted directly like in linear regression. We use the \textbf{Odds Ratio} (OR):

$$OR_j = e^{\hat{\beta}_j}$$

\vspace{0.3em}

**What is odds?**

$$\text{odds} = \frac{p}{1-p}$$

If the probability of conversion to AD is 0.25, the odds are $0.25/0.75 = 0.33$ (1 conversion for every 3 non-conversions).

\vspace{0.4em}

**What is Odds Ratio?**

It is the \emph{ratio} of odds in two conditions: treated vs untreated, or for a one-unit increase in $X$.

$$OR = \frac{\text{odds}(\text{exposed})}{\text{odds}(\text{not exposed})}$$


## The Odds Ratio: Practical Interpretation

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.85,
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick}
]
  \draw[thick,->] (0,0) -- (7,0);
  \draw[thick] (3.5,0.15)--(3.5,-0.15);
  \node[below,font=\small] at (3.5,-0.2) {OR = 1};
  \node[below,font=\footnotesize,mred] at (1.2,-0.2) {OR $<$ 1};
  \node[below,font=\footnotesize,mgreen] at (5.8,-0.2) {OR $>$ 1};
  \node[above,font=\footnotesize,mred] at (1.2,0.3) {protective factor};
  \node[above,font=\footnotesize,gray] at (3.5,0.3) {no effect};
  \node[above,font=\footnotesize,mgreen] at (5.8,0.3) {risk factor};

  % Example OR markers
  \fill[mred]   (1.5,0) circle (4pt) node[above,font=\tiny,yshift=4pt]{OR=0.4};
  \fill[gray]   (3.5,0) circle (3pt);
  \fill[mgreen] (5.2,0) circle (4pt) node[above,font=\tiny,yshift=4pt]{OR=2.5};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.2em}
```

| OR | Interpretation |
|--|--|
| \footnotesize $OR = 1$ | \footnotesize No association |
| \footnotesize $OR = 2.5$ | \footnotesize Exposed subjects have odds of the event 2.5 times those of unexposed |
| \footnotesize $OR = 0.4$ | \footnotesize Exposed subjects have odds of the event 60\% \emph{lower} than unexposed |

\vspace{0.2em}
\footnotesize \textbf{Caution}: OR $\neq$ Relative Risk (RR). The OR overestimates RR when the prevalence of the outcome is high ($>10\%$). For low prevalences, OR $\approx$ RR.

## Odds Ratio: Numerical Example

\small

Study of 200 MCI patients: 50 convert to AD within 2 years.

\vspace{0.4em}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{lcc>{\bfseries}c}
\toprule
 & \textbf{Convert} & \textbf{Do not convert} & \textbf{Total} \\
\midrule
APOE $\varepsilon4$ carriers     & 35 & 65 & 100 \\
APOE $\varepsilon4$ non-carriers & 15 & 85 & 100 \\
\midrule
\textbf{Total}                   & 50 & 150 & 200 \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.4em}
```

**Odds in carriers**: $35/65 = 0.538$

**Odds in non-carriers**: $15/85 = 0.176$

$$OR = \frac{0.538}{0.176} = \mathbf{3.06}$$

\small Carriers of APOE $\varepsilon4$ have odds of conversion \textbf{3 times higher} than non-carriers.

**Relative Risk**: $(35/100) / (15/100) = 2.33$ --- the OR overestimates because the prevalence (25\%) is not low.

## Output of a Logistic Regression (as it appears in an article)

```{=latex}
\vspace{-0.2em}
\begin{center}
\small
\textbf{Table 2.} Predictors of MCI $\to$ AD conversion at 2 years ($N = 200$; events = 50)
\vspace{0.4em}

\begin{tabular}{lrrrr}
\toprule
\textbf{Predictor} & $\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}}$ & \textbf{OR} & \textbf{95\% CI (OR)} & $\boldsymbol{p}$ \\
\midrule
Intercept            & $-3.41$ & ---  & ---              & $<.001$ \\
\rowcolor{mblue!8}
Age (years)            & $+0.09$ & 1.09 & [1.03, 1.16]     & $.004$  \\
MMSE baseline         & $-0.21$ & 0.81 & [0.72, 0.91]     & $<.001$ \\
\rowcolor{mblue!8}
APOE $\varepsilon4$   & $+1.12$ & 3.06 & [1.54, 6.08]     & $.001$  \\
Hippocampal volume    & $-0.44$ & 0.64 & [0.45, 0.91]     & $.013$  \\
Education (years)      & $-0.08$ & 0.92 & [0.81, 1.05]     & $.224$  \\
\midrule
\multicolumn{5}{l}{\footnotesize $R^2_{\text{Nagelkerke}} = .41$;\; AUC = .83;\; HL $\chi^2(8) = 6.2$, $p = .62$} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.2em}
\footnotesize
\textbf{Reading}: Each point lower on the baseline MMSE is associated with odds of conversion $1/0.81 = 1.23$ times higher (OR = 0.81 means that \emph{one point higher} is protective). APOE $\varepsilon4$ triples the odds of conversion (OR = 3.06). Education is not significant ($p = .224$).
```


## How to Read an OR from the Table

\small

**OR > 1** (e.g., APOE $\varepsilon4$, OR = 3.06):
Each unit increase in the predictor increases the odds of the outcome by a factor of 3.06.
\emph{"Carriers of APOE $\varepsilon4$ have odds of conversion approximately three times higher than non-carriers (OR = 3.06; 95\% CI [1.54, 6.08])"}

\vspace{0.4em}

**OR < 1** (e.g., MMSE, OR = 0.81):
Each unit increase in the predictor reduces the odds of the outcome.
\emph{"One point higher on baseline MMSE is associated with 19\% lower odds of conversion (OR = 0.81; 95\% CI [0.72, 0.91])"}

\vspace{0.4em}

**95\% CI that does not include 1**: significant at $\alpha = 0.05$.

\vspace{0.4em}

\textbf{Practical note}: in logistic regression, the \textbf{intercept is not interpreted} as in the linear model. It represents the log-odds of the outcome when all predictors are zero (often has no clinical sense).

## OR vs RR vs Risk Difference: When to Use Them

\small

| Measure | Formula | When to use |
|--|--|--|
| \footnotesize OR | \footnotesize $\frac{p_1/(1-p_1)}{p_0/(1-p_0)}$ | \footnotesize Case-control studies; logistic models; rare outcomes |
| \footnotesize RR | \footnotesize $p_1 / p_0$ | \footnotesize RCTs and cohort studies; outcomes with prevalence $>10\%$ |
| \footnotesize RD | \footnotesize $p_1 - p_0$ | \footnotesize Communicating absolute risk to patients |
| \footnotesize NNT | \footnotesize $1/(p_1 - p_0)$ | \footnotesize Clinical impact of a treatment |

\vspace{0.3em}
\small \textbf{Example}: OR = 3.06, but if prevalence is 10\%, RR $\approx$ 2.5 and RD $= 0.15$ (15 more cases per 100 exposed). Communicating the OR to a patient could make the effect seem larger than it is.

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Cummings (2009). The fallacy of the OR in clinical research. \emph{Am J Epidemiol} 171(6). Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1093/aje/kwp440}

# Model Selection


## The Problem of Predictor Selection

\small

With many potential predictors, how do we choose which ones to include in the final model?

\vspace{0.3em}

**Fundamental trade-off**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Too few predictors $\Rightarrow$ \emph{underfitting}: imprecise model
\item Too many predictors $\Rightarrow$ \emph{overfitting}: good on training data, does not generalize
\end{itemize}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[scale=0.82]
  \draw[thick,->] (0,0) -- (6,0) node[right,font=\small]{Model complexity};
  \draw[thick,->] (0,0) -- (0,3.5) node[above,font=\small]{Error};
  % Training error
  \draw[thick,mblue!50] plot[smooth]
    coordinates {(0.5,2.8)(1.5,1.8)(2.5,1.2)(3.5,0.8)(4.5,0.5)(5.5,0.3)};
  \node[right,font=\tiny,mblue!70] at (5.0,0.2) {Training};
  % Validation error
  \draw[thick,mblue] plot[smooth]
    coordinates {(0.5,2.8)(1.5,1.6)(2.5,1.1)(3.0,1.0)(3.5,1.1)(4.5,1.6)(5.5,2.5)};
  \node[right,font=\tiny,mblue] at (4.8,2.2) {Validation};
  % Optimal
  \draw[dashed,mred] (3.0,0) -- (3.0,3.2)
    node[above,font=\tiny,mred]{Optimal};
  \node[below,font=\tiny,mred] at (3.0,-0.15) {$p^*$};
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```


## Methods of Predictor Selection

\small

**Stepwise (forward, backward, both)**

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \emph{Forward}: start with no predictors, add one at a time the most significant
\item \emph{Backward}: start with all, remove the least significant
\item \emph{Stepwise}: combination forward + backward
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Selection based on information criteria**

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{AIC} (Akaike): penalizes for number of parameters; favors predictive models
\item \textbf{BIC} (Bayesian): penalizes more severely; tends toward more parsimonious models
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Hierarchical selection (hierarchical / theoretical)**

Predictors are added in theoretically motivated blocks, not based on statistical significance.

## Problems with Stepwise Methods

\small

Automatic stepwise is \textbf{widely used and widely criticized}:

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{itemize}\small
\item \textbf{Type I error inflation}: with many predictors tested, the probability of finding at least one false positive explodes
\item \textbf{Instability}: small variations in data produce very different final models
\item \textbf{Overestimation of effect size}: the $\hat{\beta}$ of selected predictors is systematically overestimated (\emph{winner's curse})
\item \textbf{Ignores theoretical structure}: a predictor may be excluded because it is correlated with others, not because it is irrelevant
\item \textbf{Invalid $p$-values}: post-selection $p$-values do not have their nominal interpretation
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}
\footnotesize \textbf{Practical recommendation}: prefer \textbf{theoretical/hierarchical} selection guided by hypotheses and DAGs. If stepwise is used, validate the model on an independent sample.

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Harrell (2015). \emph{Regression Modeling Strategies}. Springer. Introductory chapters Open Access: \url{https://hbiostat.org/rmsc/}

## Hierarchical Regression

\small

Predictors are entered in **blocks** ordered theoretically, and the incremental contribution of each block is assessed through the change in $R^2$ (or log-likelihood in logistic regression).

\vspace{0.3em}

**Neuropsychological example**:

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{clcc}
\toprule
\textbf{Block} & \textbf{Predictors} & $\boldsymbol{R^2}$ & $\boldsymbol{\Delta R^2}$ \\
\midrule
1 & Demographic (age, education, sex) & .18 & .18*** \\
2 & + Imaging biomarkers (hippocampal volume) & .29 & .11*** \\
3 & + Neuropsychological scores (MMSE, memory) & .41 & .12*** \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.3em}
```

\small In this logic, each block is entered \textbf{for theoretical reasons}, not because it is significant. The $\Delta R^2$ answers the question: "Do biomarkers add explanatory power beyond demographic data alone?"

# Model Diagnostics


## Diagnostics of Linear Regression

```{=latex}
% [SUGGESTED IMAGE: the 4 classic diagnostic plots from R: Residuals vs Fitted,
%  Normal Q-Q, Scale-Location, Residuals vs Leverage]
```

\small

**Residuals** ($\hat{\varepsilon}_i = Y_i - \hat{Y}_i$) must satisfy the model's assumptions. The main diagnostic plots:

\vspace{0.3em}

\begin{columns}
\column{0.5\textwidth}
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{Residuals vs Fitted}: must be random cloud (no pattern $\Rightarrow$ linearity OK, homoscedasticity OK)
\item \textbf{Normal Q-Q}: residuals must follow the diagonal (normality of residuals)
\end{itemize}
\column{0.5\textwidth}
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{Scale-Location}: to verify homoscedasticity (constant variance)
\item \textbf{Cook's distance / Leverage}: identifies influential points (\emph{outliers} that distort the line)
\end{itemize}
\end{columns}

\vspace{0.4em}
\small \textbf{In R}: \texttt{plot(model)} automatically produces these 4 plots. \texttt{car::vif(model)} computes VIF for multicollinearity.

## Diagnostics: Common Problems

\small

**Multicollinearity**: two or more predictors are highly correlated with each other.

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item Symptom: large SEs, unstable coefficients, sign changes when adding predictors
\item Diagnosis: VIF (Variance Inflation Factor) --- VIF $> 5$--$10$ indicates a problem
\item Solution: remove one of the correlated predictors, use PCA, or center variables
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Outliers and influential observations**:
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \emph{Outlier}: very large residual (subject poorly predicted)
\item \emph{Leverage}: observation with extreme value in predictors
\item \emph{Influential observation}: removing it substantially changes the model (Cook's $D > 1$)
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Heteroscedasticity**: residual variance is not constant (e.g., increases with $\hat{Y}$). Solution: transformation of $Y$ (e.g., log), or robust errors (HC).

\vspace{0.3em}

**Model specification**: non-linear relationship between $X$ and $Y$ --- add squared term or use GAM.

## Diagnostics of Logistic Regression

\small

**Calibration**: does the model predict accurate probabilities?
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{Hosmer-Lemeshow test}: divides subjects into deciles of predicted probability and compares observed vs expected frequencies. $p > .05$ indicates good calibration.
\item \textbf{Calibration plot}: predicted probability (x-axis) vs observed proportion (y-axis) --- the curve should lie close to the diagonal.
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Discrimination**: does the model distinguish cases from non-cases well?
\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item \textbf{AUC-ROC}: 0.5 = random, 1.0 = perfect. Values $> 0.75$--$0.80$ are considered acceptable in clinical practice.
\item \textbf{Sensitivity and Specificity} at optimal cut-off (e.g., Youden Index).
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.3em}

**Complete separation** (\emph{perfect separation}): if a predictor perfectly separates the two groups, the model does not converge. Solution: penalized logistic regression (Firth).

# The Problem of Predictor Inclusion

## Not All Predictors Should Be Controlled

\small

A frequent error in practice: insert into the model \textbf{all available variables} or all those correlated with the outcome, without reasoning about causal structure.

\vspace{0.4em}

As discussed in \textbf{Section 3 (DAG)}, the type of variable included in the model changes the meaning of the estimated coefficient:
\vspace{0.3em}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{>{\footnotesize}l >{\footnotesize}l >{\footnotesize}p{4.8cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Structure} & \textbf{Control?} & \textbf{Consequence if controlled} \\
\midrule
Confounder & \textbf{Yes} & Removes spurious association between $X$ and $Y$ \\
Mediator  & \textbf{Depends} & Blocks part of the causal effect of $X$ on $Y$ \\
Collider   & \textbf{No} & Introduces spurious association (selection bias) \\
Irrelevant variable & \textbf{No} & Reduces statistical power and increases SE \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
```

\vspace{0.2em}
\small $\Rightarrow$ \textbf{Model construction must start from a DAG, not from $p$-values.}\\
\small $\Rightarrow$ For further information: \textbf{Section 3}, slides on DAGs, confounders and colliders.


## The Collider in Regression: An Example

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  node/.style={circle,draw,fill=mblue!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.1cm,inner sep=1pt,align=center},
  coll/.style={circle,draw,fill=mred!20,font=\footnotesize,
               minimum size=1.1cm,inner sep=1pt,align=center},
  arr/.style={-{Stealth},mblue,thick},
  spurious/.style={-{Stealth},mred,dashed,very thick}
]
\node[node, align=center] (dep)  at (-2.8,2)  {Depression\\(X)};
\node[node, align=center] (cog)  at ( 2.8,2)  {Cognition\\(Y)};
\node[coll, align=center] (osp)  at (0,0.5)   {\textbf{Hospital}\\admission\\(Collider K)};

\draw[arr] (dep)--(osp);
\draw[arr] (cog)--(osp);
\draw[spurious] (dep) to[bend left=30]
  node[above,font=\tiny,mred, align=center]{Spurious association\\if I control K} (cog);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
\vspace{-0.3em}
```

\small Depression and cognition are independent in the general population. But both predict hospital admission. If we analyze \textbf{only hospitalized patients} (or control for hospital admission), we find an artificial \emph{negative} correlation between depression and cognition.

\vspace{0.2em}
\footnotesize This is \textbf{Berkson's bias} --- frequent in studies conducted on clinical samples.\\
$\Rightarrow$ \textbf{Section 3} for further information.


## The Rule of 10 (and 20) Events Per Predictor

\small

In logistic regression, the number of predictors that can be reliably estimated depends on the number of \textbf{events} (less frequent category), not the total number of subjects.

\vspace{0.3em}

**Classic empirical rule**: at least **10 events per predictor** (EPV = Events Per Variable).

\vspace{0.3em}

**Recent research**: the rule of 10 EPV may not be sufficient; 20 EPV or the use of shrinkage methods (Ridge, LASSO) is often recommended.

\vspace{0.4em}

**Practical example**:

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize
\item $N = 200$ MCI subjects, 40 convert to AD (EPV = 40)
\item With EPV = 40 we can reliably estimate up to $\sim$4 predictors (rule of 10)
\item Including 8 would cause overfitting even with $N = 200$
\end{itemize}

\vspace{0.2em}
\tiny Riley et al.\ (2019). Minimum sample size for developing a multivariable prediction model. \emph{Stat Med}. Open Access: \url{https://doi.org/10.1002/sim.7993}

---

# Summary

## Summary: Linear vs Logistic

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tabular}{>{\small}l >{\small}l >{\small}l}
\toprule
 & \textbf{Linear Regression} & \textbf{Logistic Regression} \\
\midrule
Outcome    & Continuous ($Y \in \mathbb{R}$) & Binary ($Y \in \{0,1\}$) \\
Estimation      & Least squares (OLS) & Maximum likelihood \\
Coefficient & $\hat{\beta}$: change in $Y$ & $\hat{\beta}$: change in log-odds \\
Effect    & $\hat{\beta}$ direct & OR = $e^{\hat{\beta}}$ \\
Fit        & $R^2$, RMSE & AUC, pseudo-$R^2$, HL \\
Diagnostics & Residuals, Q-Q, Cook's $D$ & Calibration, separation \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}
\vspace{0.5em}
\small \textbf{Golden rule common to both}: build the model based on theoretical hypotheses and DAGs, not on automatic stepwise or statistical significance.
```

## Open Access Resources

\small

\begin{itemize}\footnotesize

\item \textbf{Harrell (2015). Regression Modeling Strategies}. Online materials:\\
  \url{https://hbiostat.org/rmsc/}

\item \textbf{Riley et al.\ (2019). Sample size for prediction models}. \emph{Stat Med}.\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1002/sim.7993}

\item \textbf{OR vs RR}: Cummings (2009). \emph{Am J Epidemiol} 171(6).\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1093/aje/kwp440}

\item \textbf{LASSO and Ridge}: Tibshirani (1996). \emph{J R Stat Soc B}. (Fundamental for shrinkage)\\
  \url{https://doi.org/10.1111/j.2517-6161.1996.tb02080.x}

\item \textbf{R package \texttt{rms}} (Harrell): advanced regression, calibration, validation.\\
  \url{https://cran.r-project.org/package=rms}

\item \textbf{DAGitty}: for building DAGs before choosing predictors.\\
  \url{https://www.dagitty.net}

\item \textbf{Sievert (2020). Interactive Web-Based Data Visualization with R} (plotly, ggplot2).\\
  Open Access book: \url{https://plotly-r.com}

\item \textbf{The Effect Book (Huntington-Klein)}: chapters on OLS and logistic regression.\\
  \url{https://theeffectbook.net}

\end{itemize}
